one_line_takeaway: A VTS 2026 special-session survey of AI/LLM-assisted hardware security verification paired with a scoped NVDLA CSB-master case study showing an LLM-guided agentic pipeline can identify a real CWE-1220 access-control gap (core_req_prdy permanently high) and confirm it via simulation (30/31 test transactions forwarded without privilege check), while explicitly declining to claim system-level exploitability.
key_finding: Of 31 exercised transactions in simulation, 30 were observed forwarded to decoded sub-unit ports and flagged as security violations (29 writes and 1 read). The remaining transaction, targeting the unmapped address 0x7C00, was accepted at ingress but routed to an internal dummy client not externally monitored. Across all valid requests, the ready signal never deasserted, indicating zero back-pressure/throttling in the exercised cases (Test 5, rapid-fire x8). The authors conclude that at this module boundary the CSB master accepts and forwards the tested requests without a local privilege check, and recommend three mitigations: (R1) enforce interconnect-level access control before the module, (R2) add privilege/source-ID metadata for local authorization, (R3) gate core_req_prdy on an authorization check.
core_contribution: The paper has two own contributions, clearly separated in the text. (1) A survey that organizes recent AI/LLM-assisted hardware security verification literature into five workflow stages -- asset identification, threat modeling, security test-plan generation, simulation-driven/formal verification, and countermeasure reasoning -- and poses three open research questions (end-to-end automation, grounding to reduce hallucination, and benchmarks/evaluation criteria). (2) An original case study applying an 'automated agentic workflow' (the paper's own pipeline, not a cited tool) to the open-source NVIDIA Deep Learning Accelerator (NVDLA), specifically the NV_NVDLA_csb_master IP (public nvdlav1 snapshot), producing LLM-generated asset lists, a threat model, a directed test plan, simulation results, and countermeasure recommendations for that single module boundary.
threat_model: Scoped to the CSB (Configuration Space Bus) master module boundary only, not a system-level claim. Threat: an untrusted/unauthorized requester reaching the CSB master can issue read/write requests that are accepted and forwarded to decoded sub-unit paths without any local privilege check, because core_req_prdy is permanently asserted (RTL line 468) and no privilege/source-ID field exists in the 50-bit request packet ({nposted, write, addr[15:0], wdata[31:0]}). Mapped to CWE-1220 (Insufficient Granularity of Access Control) and STRIDE categories Tampering, Information Disclosure, and Elevation of Privilege. The authors explicitly state the case study 'does not claim that every NVDLA deployment is exploitable' -- it evaluates only whether the CSB master in isolation lacks local privilege enforcement, leaving system-level exploitability dependent on SoC integration and reachability.
limitations: The authors themselves scope the finding tightly: the case study covers a single IP (CSB master) and a single CWE class (CWE-1220); it is explicitly stated as 'module boundary only; downstream effects not claimed,' and whether the finding becomes a system-level vulnerability 'depends on SoC integration and reachability of the CSB path.' No comparison is made against non-LLM/manual verification baselines, no hallucination/false-positive rate is quantified for the LLM-generated assets, threat model, or tests, and the 'automated agentic workflow' used for the case study is not named or architecturally detailed beyond being agentic and LLM-driven. The survey identifies (rather than resolves) open problems around grounding, benchmarks, and accelerator-specific abstractions (RQ2, RQ3), i.e. the paper itself does not propose or evaluate a hallucination-mitigation or benchmarking solution.

datasets_used: Open-source NVIDIA Deep Learning Accelerator (NVDLA), nvdlav1 public snapshot, NV_NVDLA_csb_master IP
quantitative_results: 30 of 31 transactions observed at decoded sub-unit ports and flagged as security violations (29 writes, 1 read)
1 transaction (targeting unmapped address 0x7C00) accepted at ingress but routed to an unmonitored internal dummy client
Ready signal never deasserted across all valid requests (zero back-pressure observed under rapid-fire x8 test)
5 assets identified for the CSB master (Table I), each mapped to 3 CWE IDs
5-item directed test plan (Table III): MCIF write, BDMA/CVIF/CDMA write, MCIF read, full 18-target address scan, rapid-fire x8
